ip_provisions.name: Full IP Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Complete transfer of all intellectual property rights in work product to the receiving party, covering patents, copyrights, trade secrets, and all related rights worldwide.
drafting_checklist: Define scope of assigned IP precisely
Specify territory (worldwide vs limited)
Address moral rights (waiver or consent)
Include reversionary rights on non-use or breach
Require execution of confirmatory assignments
Cover future IP arising from assigned work
Address employee/contractor inventor obligations
Include representations of non-encumbrance
Specify consideration and payment terms
Address pre-existing/background IP exclusions

buyer_risks: Assignment may not cover all IP types in all jurisdictions
Moral rights cannot be assigned in civil law countries
Prior encumbrances may limit assigned rights
Employee inventions may require separate assignment
seller_risks: Loss of ability to reuse any work product
May inadvertently assign background IP
No right to use improvements or derivatives
Potential loss of competitive advantage

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Moral rights cannot be waived or assigned in most EU member states under Berne Convention
jurisdiction_flags.US: Work-for-hire doctrine may apply for employees; independent contractor assignments require written agreement per 17 USC 204
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Moral rights can be waived but not assigned under CDPA 1988; paternity right must be asserted

ip_provisions.name: Limited IP Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Transfer of specific, enumerated intellectual property rights while retaining others. Commonly used for defined deliverables in project-based engagements.
drafting_checklist: Enumerate specific IP being assigned
Define what is expressly excluded
Specify field-of-use limitations
Address derivative works ownership
Include clear boundary definitions
Require IP schedule or exhibit
Address improvements during warranty period
Specify geographic limitations if any

buyer_risks: Gaps in assigned rights may limit exploitation
Retained rights may compete with assigned rights
Unclear boundaries create enforcement difficulties
seller_risks: Scope creep may expand assignment beyond intent
Derivative works clause may capture retained IP
Field-of-use limitations may be construed broadly

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Partial assignments recognized but must comply with national IP registration requirements
jurisdiction_flags.US: Partial copyright assignments valid; patent assignments should be recorded with USPTO
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Partial assignments valid but beneficial ownership complexities may arise

ip_provisions.name: Future IP Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Pre-assignment of intellectual property that has not yet been created, typically covering work product to be developed during the contract term.
drafting_checklist: Define triggering events for assignment
Specify temporal scope clearly
Include automatic assignment language
Require confirmatory assignment documents
Address invention disclosure obligations
Cover improvements and enhancements
Include cooperation clause for filings
Address consideration for future IP

buyer_risks: Future IP assignments may be unenforceable in some jurisdictions
Scope may not capture all intended IP
Creator may dispute what falls within scope
seller_risks: Overly broad scope may capture unrelated innovations
May conflict with obligations to other parties
Reduced incentive for innovation

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Future copyright assignment validity varies by member state; Germany restricts assignment of future works under UrhG
jurisdiction_flags.US: Generally enforceable; automatic assignment language preferred per Stanford v Roche
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Equitable assignment of future IP recognized; legal assignment requires writing

ip_provisions.name: Assignment with License-Back
ip_provisions.description: Full assignment of IP to the buyer combined with a perpetual license back to the seller, allowing continued use of transferred IP in non-competing fields.
drafting_checklist: Define scope of assignment clearly
Specify license-back scope and limitations
Define non-competing fields precisely
Address sublicensing rights under license-back
Specify whether license-back is royalty-free
Include field-of-use restrictions
Address modifications and derivatives under license
Specify termination conditions for license-back

buyer_risks: License-back may reduce exclusivity value
Seller may compete indirectly through licensed use
Sublicensing rights may dilute IP value
seller_risks: License-back scope may be too narrow for business needs
Non-compete field definitions may be construed broadly
License-back may terminate on breach or change of control

jurisdiction_flags.EU: License-back structures generally recognized; competition law may limit field-of-use restrictions under TFEU Art 101
jurisdiction_flags.US: Valid structure; antitrust review may apply if parties are competitors
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Recognized; restrictive covenant principles may apply to field-of-use limitations

ip_provisions.name: Conditional IP Assignment
ip_provisions.description: IP assignment that takes effect only upon occurrence of specified conditions such as full payment, project completion, or regulatory approval.
drafting_checklist: Define triggering conditions precisely
Specify ownership during conditional period
Address risk of loss during interim
Include automatic transfer mechanism
Define remedies if conditions not met
Address interim license rights
Specify deadline for condition fulfillment
Include escrow provisions if appropriate

buyer_risks: Conditions may never be satisfied
Interim period creates ownership uncertainty
Seller may encumber IP before transfer
seller_risks: May be unable to exploit IP during conditional period
Buyer may delay condition fulfillment
Reversion rights may be difficult to exercise

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Conditional assignments recognized; registration timing may create third-party rights issues
jurisdiction_flags.US: Equitable interests may arise before legal assignment; recording with USPTO recommended
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Equitable assignment arises on condition; Stamp Duty considerations on transfer

ip_provisions.name: Employee Invention Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Assignment of inventions and IP created by employees during employment, typically as part of employment agreement. Subject to significant statutory variation by jurisdiction.
drafting_checklist: Define scope of assigned inventions
Exclude personal inventions made outside employment
Comply with state-specific invention assignment laws
Include invention disclosure obligations
Address prior inventions schedule
Specify compensation for service inventions if required
Include cooperation clause for patent prosecution
Address post-employment inventions holdover period
Comply with mandatory employee protections

buyer_risks: Statutory limitations may narrow assignable scope
Employees may dispute invention ownership
Missing prior invention disclosures create ambiguity
Inadequate consideration may void assignment
seller_risks: Overly broad scope may capture personal inventions
Holdover period may restrict post-employment activities
Failure to disclose prior inventions may create liability

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Service invention regimes vary significantly: Germany requires reasonable compensation under ArbNErfG; France distinguishes mission inventions from off-mission inventions
jurisdiction_flags.US: State laws limit scope: California Labor Code 2870 protects personal inventions; similar laws in DE, IL, MN, NC, WA
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Patents Act 1977 s39-42 governs employee inventions; employees may claim compensation for outstanding benefit patents

ip_provisions.name: Confirmatory IP Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Supplementary assignment document executed to confirm and perfect a prior assignment, often used to address specific IP registrations or jurisdictional requirements.
drafting_checklist: Reference original assignment agreement
Identify specific IP rights being confirmed
Include all required formalities for jurisdiction
Execute in form suitable for registration
Address chain of title issues
Include notarization if required
Cover all relevant IP offices
Specify recording responsibilities

buyer_risks: Delay in execution may create priority issues
Original assignment defects may not be cured
May require additional consideration in some jurisdictions
seller_risks: Obligation to execute may persist indefinitely
May be required to travel or appear for notarization
Cost of execution and recording

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Required for patent/trademark registration transfers at national and EPO/EUIPO level
jurisdiction_flags.US: Recording with USPTO/Copyright Office recommended but not required for validity
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Required for legal assignment; IPO recording establishes priority

ip_provisions.name: Partial Copyright Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Assignment of specific exclusive rights within the copyright bundle while retaining others, such as assigning reproduction rights while retaining public performance rights.
drafting_checklist: Enumerate specific rights being assigned from copyright bundle
Specify retained rights explicitly
Define media and format limitations
Address digital vs physical exploitation
Specify territory for each assigned right
Include reversion on non-exploitation
Address collective licensing implications
Specify duration if less than full copyright term

buyer_risks: Retained rights may limit commercial exploitation
Territorial limitations may create enforcement gaps
Digital rights scope may be disputed
seller_risks: Assigned rights may be broader than intended
Reversion clauses may be difficult to trigger
Collective licensing societies may complicate retained rights

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Copyright bundles vary by member state; moral rights retained regardless of assignment
jurisdiction_flags.US: Copyright Act permits divisibility of copyright; each exclusive right independently owned per 17 USC 201(d)
jurisdiction_flags.UK: CDPA 1988 permits partial assignment by right, territory, or duration

ip_provisions.name: Trademark Assignment with Goodwill
ip_provisions.description: Transfer of trademark rights together with the associated goodwill of the business, required in most jurisdictions to avoid invalid assignment in gross.
drafting_checklist: Identify all trademarks being assigned (registrations, applications, common law)
Include assignment of associated goodwill
Transfer domain names associated with marks
Address pending applications and oppositions
Include quality control transition provisions
Specify recording obligations at trademark offices
Address use during transition period
Cover social media handles and digital assets
Include representations of validity and non-infringement

buyer_risks: Goodwill may be difficult to value
Third-party licenses may restrict use
Pending oppositions or cancellations may succeed
Mark may have been abandoned or weakened
seller_risks: Loss of brand identity and customer recognition
Buyer misuse may damage residual goodwill
Non-compete obligations may limit rebranding

jurisdiction_flags.EU: EUTM assignments can be for all or some goods/services; must be recorded at EUIPO
jurisdiction_flags.US: Assignment in gross (without goodwill) is invalid; must include tangible assets or ongoing business
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Must be in writing and signed; recording at UKIPO recommended for priority

ip_provisions.name: Patent Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Transfer of patent rights including issued patents and pending applications, covering the right to make, use, sell, and enforce the patented technology.
drafting_checklist: List all patents and applications by number and jurisdiction
Include continuations, divisionals, and reissues
Address prosecution rights and obligations
Specify enforcement rights and standing
Include representations of validity and ownership
Address maintenance fee responsibilities during transition
Cover foreign counterparts and PCT applications
Include cooperation clause for prosecution
Specify recording at relevant patent offices
Address inventor obligations if separate from assignor

buyer_risks: Patents may be invalid or unenforceable
Prior licenses may limit exclusivity
Prosecution history may limit claim scope
Maintenance fees must be paid to avoid lapse
seller_risks: Loss of defensive patent portfolio
May need patent back-license for retained business
Continuing inventor cooperation may be burdensome

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Must be recorded at each national patent office and/or EPO; unitary patent simplifies
jurisdiction_flags.US: Must be recorded at USPTO within 3 months per 35 USC 261; constructive notice only if recorded
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Must be registered at UKIPO; unregistered assignment is not evidence of title

ip_provisions.name: Design Rights Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Transfer of registered and unregistered design rights covering the visual appearance of products, including industrial designs, design patents, and community designs.
drafting_checklist: Identify all registered and unregistered designs
Specify jurisdictions covered
Address design patent equivalents
Include CAD files and design documentation
Cover supplementary unregistered design rights
Address grace periods for unregistered designs
Specify recording at design offices
Include representations of originality

buyer_risks: Unregistered designs have limited duration
Originality may be challenged
Prior art may invalidate registered designs
seller_risks: Loss of product differentiation
Transfer of design documentation may reveal trade secrets
Residual unregistered rights may be inadvertently transferred

jurisdiction_flags.EU: EU registered community designs (RCDs) assigned at EUIPO; unregistered community designs last 3 years
jurisdiction_flags.US: Design patents assigned same as utility patents; trade dress may provide additional protection
jurisdiction_flags.UK: UK registered designs assigned at UKIPO; supplementary unregistered design right lasts 15 years

ip_provisions.name: Database Rights Assignment
ip_provisions.description: Transfer of sui generis database rights protecting substantial investment in obtaining, verifying, or presenting database contents. Primarily an EU/UK right.
drafting_checklist: Identify the database and its contents
Specify whether copyright in structure also assigned
Address ongoing data updates and maintenance
Include data quality representations
Cover extraction and re-utilization rights
Address database maker identification
Specify territory (EU/EEA for sui generis right)
Include data migration assistance obligations

buyer_risks: Database rights do not exist in all jurisdictions
Rights may expire (15 years from completion)
Underlying data may have third-party restrictions
seller_risks: Loss of data asset and competitive intelligence
May need license-back for retained operations
Ongoing update obligations may be burdensome

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Sui generis database right under Database Directive 96/9/EC; 15-year term renewable on substantial update
jurisdiction_flags.US: No sui generis database right; protection via copyright (creative selection/arrangement), contract, or trade secret
jurisdiction_flags.UK: UK retained EU database right post-Brexit; separate UK right continues

ip_provisions.name: Exclusive Copyright License
ip_provisions.description: Grant of exclusive rights to reproduce, distribute, display, perform, and create derivative works from copyrighted material within specified scope.
drafting_checklist: Define licensed rights from copyright bundle
Specify exclusivity scope and territory
Include or exclude sublicensing rights
Define permitted derivative works
Specify royalty structure or lump sum
Address minimum performance obligations
Include reversion on non-exploitation
Specify reporting and audit rights
Address moral rights acknowledgment
Define termination triggers

buyer_risks: Exclusive license may not convey standing to sue in all jurisdictions
Author may retain moral rights that limit exploitation
Sublicensing restrictions may limit business models
seller_risks: Cannot exploit or license to others in exclusive field
Revenue dependent on licensee performance
Reversion clauses may be difficult to exercise

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Exclusive licensee may have standing to sue in some member states; mandatory fair compensation rules may apply
jurisdiction_flags.US: Exclusive licensee has standing to sue under 17 USC 501(b); must be in writing per 17 USC 204
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Exclusive licensee has concurrent rights with copyright owner under CDPA 1988 s101

ip_provisions.name: Exclusive Patent License
ip_provisions.description: Grant of exclusive rights to make, use, sell, offer for sale, and import patented technology within defined fields of use and territories.
drafting_checklist: Identify licensed patents by number and jurisdiction
Define field of use precisely
Specify exclusive territory
Address sublicensing rights and conditions
Define royalty structure (running, lump sum, minimum)
Include diligence/best efforts obligations
Address improvement patents
Specify enforcement rights and cost-sharing
Include milestone obligations if applicable
Address patent prosecution cooperation

buyer_risks: Patent may be invalidated
Field of use may be construed narrowly
Improvement patents may not be included
Enforcement costs may be substantial
seller_risks: Cannot practice or license in exclusive field
Revenue dependent on licensee commercialization
May lose control over enforcement strategy

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Technology transfer agreements subject to TTBER block exemption; exclusive territory/field valid within limits
jurisdiction_flags.US: Exclusive licensee generally has standing to sue; some circuits require all substantial rights
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Exclusive licensee has right to bring proceedings jointly with patentee under Patents Act 1977 s67

ip_provisions.name: Exclusive Trademark License
ip_provisions.description: Grant of exclusive rights to use trademarks in specified territories and for specified goods/services, with mandatory quality control provisions.
drafting_checklist: Identify licensed trademarks precisely
Define licensed territory
Specify licensed goods and services
Include quality control standards
Address quality monitoring and inspection rights
Specify royalty or franchise fee structure
Include use guidelines and brand standards
Address sublicensing restrictions
Specify enforcement obligations
Include minimum sales/marketing obligations

buyer_risks: Quality control obligations may be burdensome
Territory restrictions may limit growth
Trademark may be challenged or cancelled
seller_risks: Inadequate quality control may constitute naked license
Licensee misuse may damage brand globally
Revenue concentration risk

jurisdiction_flags.EU: EUTM licenses can be EU-wide or territorial; quality control required to avoid abandonment
jurisdiction_flags.US: Licensor must exercise quality control or risk naked license doctrine; Lanham Act requirements
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Must be recorded at UKIPO; licensee has limited enforcement rights under Trade Marks Act 1994

ip_provisions.name: Exclusive Technology License
ip_provisions.description: Comprehensive exclusive license covering a technology package including patents, know-how, trade secrets, and technical documentation for commercialization.
drafting_checklist: Define technology package comprehensively
Enumerate all included IP rights
Include technical documentation and training
Specify field of use and territory
Address improvement sharing obligations
Define royalty structure with minimums
Include commercialization milestones
Specify audit rights for royalty verification
Address technology updates and enhancements
Include non-compete on licensor in exclusive field

buyer_risks: Technology may become obsolete
Know-how difficult to return on termination
Licensor may compete indirectly
Integration with own technology may be complex
seller_risks: Loss of technology commercialization in exclusive field
Know-how once transferred cannot be un-disclosed
Revenue dependent on licensee success
May lose talent to licensee

jurisdiction_flags.EU: TTBER applies; hard-core restrictions on price fixing, output limitation, market allocation between competitors
jurisdiction_flags.US: Antitrust scrutiny under Sherman Act for competitor licenses; DOJ/FTC IP Licensing Guidelines apply
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Competition Act 1998 Chapter I prohibition mirrors EU approach; CMA guidance on IP licensing

ip_provisions.name: Sole License
ip_provisions.description: License where the licensor agrees not to grant further licenses but retains the right to use the IP itself. Distinguished from exclusive license where licensor also cannot use.
drafting_checklist: Clearly distinguish from exclusive license
Define licensor retained rights precisely
Specify whether licensor can sublicense retained rights
Address licensor use limitations
Define licensee exclusive commercial rights
Specify enforcement rights allocation
Include most-favored-licensee provisions
Address improvement rights

buyer_risks: Licensor may compete using retained rights
Less exclusivity than full exclusive license
Enforcement standing may be limited
seller_risks: Cannot grant additional licenses
Restricted to own-use only
May face pressure to convert to exclusive

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Sole license concept recognized; treated differently from exclusive license under TTBER
jurisdiction_flags.US: Distinct from exclusive license for standing purposes; retained rights may affect exclusivity analysis
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Sole license distinguished from exclusive license; different enforcement rights under Patents Act 1977

ip_provisions.name: Time-Limited Exclusive License
ip_provisions.description: Exclusive license for a defined period, after which exclusivity converts to non-exclusive or terminates, balancing commercialization incentive with IP flexibility.
drafting_checklist: Define exclusivity period precisely
Specify conversion terms after exclusivity period
Include performance milestones during exclusive period
Address early termination of exclusivity
Define non-exclusive terms post-conversion
Include minimum royalty obligations
Address sub-licensee rights on conversion
Specify notice requirements for conversion

buyer_risks: Exclusivity period may be insufficient for ROI
Competitors may prepare for non-exclusive period
Investment in market building benefits later non-exclusive licensees
seller_risks: Cannot grant other licenses during exclusive period
Conversion may be difficult to administer
Market expectations set during exclusive period

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Time-limited exclusivity generally acceptable under TTBER; may extend to vertical agreements
jurisdiction_flags.US: Common in pharmaceutical licensing; Hatch-Waxman considerations for drug patents
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Acceptable structure; competition law applies to duration assessment

ip_provisions.name: Geographically Exclusive License
ip_provisions.description: Exclusive license limited to specific territories, allowing the licensor to grant separate exclusives in different geographic markets.
drafting_checklist: Define territories precisely using recognized boundaries
Address online/digital sales across territory borders
Include anti-circumvention provisions
Address parallel imports
Specify local registration requirements
Include local enforcement obligations
Address cross-border customer sales
Define territory expansion mechanisms

buyer_risks: Territory may be too narrow for efficient operations
Cross-border digital sales may erode exclusivity
Parallel imports may undermine territorial protection
seller_risks: Different licensees may deliver inconsistent quality
Territory disputes may arise at borders
Administration of multiple territorial licenses is complex

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Territorial exclusivity limited by free movement of goods; passive sales cannot be restricted per Regulation 316/2014
jurisdiction_flags.US: Territorial restrictions generally permissible; antitrust analysis under rule of reason
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Post-Brexit, separate territory from EU; parallel import rules may differ from EU approach

ip_provisions.name: Field-of-Use Exclusive License
ip_provisions.description: Exclusive license limited to specific applications, industries, or markets, allowing the licensor to grant separate exclusives for different fields of use.
drafting_checklist: Define field of use with technical precision
Address emerging applications and convergence
Include dispute resolution for boundary cases
Specify classification methodology for new uses
Address multi-field products
Include non-compete within licensed field
Specify sub-field grant-back rights
Address technology convergence scenarios

buyer_risks: Field may be defined too narrowly
Technology convergence may blur field boundaries
Competitor licensees in adjacent fields may encroach
seller_risks: Field boundaries may be litigated
New applications may not fit existing field definitions
Grant-back obligations may limit retained fields

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Field-of-use restrictions valid under TTBER for non-competing undertakings; competing undertakings limited
jurisdiction_flags.US: Valid per Quanta v LG Electronics analysis; patent exhaustion may limit post-sale restrictions
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Generally valid; CMA may scrutinize if fields overlap significantly

ip_provisions.name: Non-Exclusive Copyright License
ip_provisions.description: Grant of non-exclusive rights to use copyrighted material, allowing licensor to grant similar rights to multiple licensees simultaneously.
drafting_checklist: Define licensed rights precisely
Specify permitted uses and formats
Address digital distribution rights
Specify territory and duration
Define sublicensing rights if any
Include attribution requirements
Address modification rights
Specify reporting obligations
Include audit rights for royalty verification
Address termination and post-termination use

buyer_risks: Competitors may hold identical licenses
No exclusivity protection
Licensor may grant preferential terms to others
seller_risks: Revenue may be lower than exclusive license
Multiple licensees increase administration
Market saturation may reduce individual licensee value

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Non-exclusive licenses do not require writing in all member states but recommended; territorial restrictions subject to geo-blocking regulation
jurisdiction_flags.US: Non-exclusive copyright licenses can be oral or implied; writing recommended for clarity
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Non-exclusive licenses valid without writing; exclusive licensee rights do not apply

ip_provisions.name: Non-Exclusive Patent License
ip_provisions.description: Grant of non-exclusive rights to practice patented technology, permitting multiple licensees to operate under the same patent portfolio.
drafting_checklist: Identify licensed patents precisely
Define scope of licensed activities
Specify have-made rights
Address patent family scope
Define royalty structure
Include most-favored-licensee clause
Address defensive termination provisions
Specify indemnification obligations
Include patent marking requirements
Address cross-licensing potential

buyer_risks: No exclusivity; competitors may be licensed
Licensor may grant better terms to competitors
Have-made rights scope may be limited
seller_risks: Multiple licensees may commoditize technology
Administrative burden of multiple agreements
Revenue optimization requires careful pricing

jurisdiction_flags.EU: TTBER safe harbor applies; no restriction on number of non-exclusive licensees
jurisdiction_flags.US: Non-exclusive patent licenses are personal and generally non-transferable unless agreed; implied license doctrine may apply
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Non-exclusive patent licensee has limited enforcement rights under Patents Act 1977

ip_provisions.name: Non-Exclusive Software License
ip_provisions.description: Standard software license granting non-exclusive rights to use, copy, and display software, typically for internal business purposes with restrictions on modification and redistribution.
drafting_checklist: Define permitted number of users or installations
Specify permitted use (internal business only)
Address SaaS vs on-premise deployment
Include or exclude source code access
Restrict reverse engineering and decompilation
Define backup copy rights
Address virtualization and cloud deployment
Specify maintenance and support terms
Include escrow provisions if applicable
Address audit rights for compliance

buyer_risks: Vendor lock-in without source code access
License metrics may cause unexpected costs
Decompilation restrictions may impede integration
seller_risks: Unauthorized copying difficult to detect
License metrics may be difficult to enforce
Open source alternatives may erode value

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Software Directive 2009/24/EC permits decompilation for interoperability; UsedSoft ruling permits resale of downloaded copies
jurisdiction_flags.US: EULA enforceability varies by state; DMCA anti-circumvention applies; first sale doctrine limited for licensed software
jurisdiction_flags.UK: CDPA 1988 s50A-C permits decompilation for interoperability and backup; resale rights uncertain post-Brexit

ip_provisions.name: Non-Exclusive Trademark Use License
ip_provisions.description: Permission to use trademarks in connection with approved products or services, subject to quality control and brand guidelines.
drafting_checklist: Identify licensed marks precisely
Define approved products and services
Include quality control standards and procedures
Provide brand usage guidelines
Address co-branding and attribution
Specify approval process for materials
Include termination for quality failures
Address post-termination wind-down of branded materials
Specify whether licensee can register own marks

buyer_risks: Quality control may be burdensome
Brand guidelines may change frequently
Other licensees may damage brand reputation
seller_risks: Multiple licensees increase quality control burden
Naked licensing risk if quality not monitored
Brand dilution from multiple users

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Quality control mandatory to maintain EUTM rights; naked licensing may lead to revocation
jurisdiction_flags.US: Lanham Act requires quality control; naked license may result in abandonment of mark
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Quality control required; Trade Marks Act 1994 provisions on licensing

ip_provisions.name: Non-Exclusive Data License
ip_provisions.description: License to access, use, and process specified datasets for defined purposes, addressing data protection, derived data ownership, and usage restrictions.
drafting_checklist: Define licensed data precisely
Specify permitted processing activities
Address data protection compliance (GDPR/CCPA)
Define derived data ownership
Restrict data combination with other sources
Include data quality warranties
Specify data refresh and update obligations
Address data retention and deletion on termination
Include security requirements for data handling
Define anonymization/pseudonymization requirements

buyer_risks: Data quality may deteriorate over time
Derived data ownership may be disputed
Personal data may trigger regulatory obligations
seller_risks: Data once shared is difficult to control
Licensee may extract value beyond licensed scope
Competing data products may be created

jurisdiction_flags.EU: GDPR applies to personal data; Database Directive 96/9/EC protects substantial investment; Data Act 2024 affects data sharing obligations
jurisdiction_flags.US: No comprehensive federal data protection; sectoral laws (CCPA, HIPAA) may apply; trade secret protection available
jurisdiction_flags.UK: UK GDPR applies to personal data; database right retained post-Brexit; data adequacy decision with EU

ip_provisions.name: Non-Exclusive Content License
ip_provisions.description: License to use, reproduce, and distribute content including text, images, video, and multimedia across specified channels and formats.
drafting_checklist: Define content scope precisely
Specify licensed channels and platforms
Address format conversion rights
Include or exclude editing and adaptation rights
Define attribution requirements
Specify exclusivity periods if any
Address user-generated content incorporation
Include content takedown procedures
Specify archival rights post-termination
Address AI training use of content

buyer_risks: Content may contain third-party IP
Attribution requirements may be impractical
Format limitations may restrict distribution
seller_risks: Content may be used beyond licensed scope
AI training use may not have been contemplated
Revenue dilution from multiple licensees

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Copyright Directive 2019/790 affects text and data mining rights; AI Act may affect AI training use
jurisdiction_flags.US: Fair use may limit content control; DMCA safe harbors for platforms; AI training use unsettled
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Fair dealing exceptions more limited than US fair use; AI training exception introduced then paused

ip_provisions.name: API License
ip_provisions.description: License to access and integrate with application programming interfaces, defining usage limits, rate limits, data rights, and acceptable use policies.
drafting_checklist: Define API endpoints and documentation access
Specify rate limits and usage quotas
Address API versioning and deprecation
Define data ownership for API responses
Include uptime and performance commitments
Restrict competitive use of API data
Address caching and storage of API data
Include security requirements for API keys
Specify acceptable use policy
Address derivative application ownership

buyer_risks: API changes may break integrations
Rate limits may constrain scaling
Vendor lock-in through proprietary API
API deprecation with insufficient notice
seller_risks: API abuse may impact platform performance
Screen scraping may bypass API controls
Competitive applications built on API
Data extraction beyond intended scope

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Database Directive and Data Act may affect API data extraction rights; interoperability requirements under DMA
jurisdiction_flags.US: CFAA implications for exceeding authorized access; API terms generally enforceable; Oracle v Google on API copyrightability
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Computer Misuse Act applies to unauthorized access; API terms enforceable under contract law

ip_provisions.name: Non-Exclusive Music License
ip_provisions.description: License to use musical works and sound recordings in specified media, addressing synchronization rights, mechanical rights, and performance rights.
drafting_checklist: Identify musical works and recordings precisely
Specify sync vs mechanical vs performance rights
Define permitted media and platforms
Address territory limitations
Specify duration and renewal terms
Include credits and attribution
Address streaming and digital distribution
Specify remix and sampling rights
Include cue sheet obligations
Address collective society reporting

buyer_risks: Multiple rights holders may need separate clearances
Collective society claims may arise
Streaming platforms may require direct licenses
seller_risks: Non-exclusive grants reduce per-license revenue
Competing uses may diminish work value
Collective society membership may restrict direct licensing

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Collective rights management under CRM Directive 2014/26/EU; multi-territory licensing for online use required
jurisdiction_flags.US: Mechanical licenses compulsory under Section 115; PRO (ASCAP/BMI/SESAC) administer performance rights
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Collecting societies (PRS, PPL, MCPS) manage most rights; Hargreaves reforms on copyright licensing

ip_provisions.name: Brand Endorsement License
ip_provisions.description: License to use an endorser or influencer name, image, and likeness in connection with products or services, addressing publicity rights and content approval.
drafting_checklist: Define licensed name, image, and likeness rights
Specify approved products and campaigns
Include content pre-approval process
Address social media posting obligations
Define exclusivity within category if any
Specify morality and conduct clauses
Address AI-generated likeness rights
Include FTC/ASA disclosure requirements
Specify post-termination use wind-down
Define performance metrics and incentives

buyer_risks: Endorser controversy may damage brand
Performance obligations may not be met
Likeness rights scope may be disputed
seller_risks: Brand association may limit other opportunities
AI deepfake risk with licensed likeness
Morality clause termination may be subjective

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Right of publicity varies by member state; GDPR applies to personal data; AI Act may affect synthetic media
jurisdiction_flags.US: Right of publicity under state law (varies significantly); FTC Endorsement Guides require disclosure; Section 43(a) Lanham Act
jurisdiction_flags.UK: No standalone right of publicity; passing off and data protection provide limited protection

ip_provisions.name: Academic/Research License

ip_provisions.name: Open Data and Database Open Licensing
ip_provisions.description: Open licensing provisions for databases and datasets including Open Data Commons licenses (ODbL, ODC-By, PDDL), Creative Commons for data, and sui generis database rights interaction with open licenses. Covers data quality obligations, attribution stacking, and share-alike requirements for derived datasets.
drafting_checklist: Select appropriate open data license (ODbL, CC-BY, CC0, PDDL)
Define scope: data contents vs. database structure vs. both
Address sui generis database right waiver or licensing
Specify attribution requirements and stacking mechanism for derivatives
Define share-alike scope for produced works vs. derivative databases
Address data quality and accuracy disclaimers
Specify update and maintenance obligations for published datasets
Define API access terms consistent with open data license
Address personal data exclusion and privacy compliance
Specify compatibility with other open data licenses

buyer_risks: Share-alike requirements may contaminate proprietary database products
Attribution stacking becomes impractical for large aggregated datasets
Data quality may degrade without maintenance obligations
License incompatibility between different open data sources
seller_risks: Open licensing eliminates ability to monetize database commercially
Data quality issues in open release may damage institutional reputation
Personal data inadvertently included may create GDPR liability
Competitors gain equal access to expensive-to-collect datasets

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Sui generis database right (Directive 96/9/EC) requires separate licensing; GDPR applies to personal data in open datasets; Open Data Directive for public sector; Data Act implications for IoT data
jurisdiction_flags.US: No sui generis database right; Feist Publications limits copyright in data compilations; hot news misappropriation may apply; CCPA/CPRA for personal data in datasets
jurisdiction_flags.UK: UK retained sui generis database right post-Brexit; Open Government Licence for public sector data; ICO anonymization guidance; crown copyright provisions for government data

ip_provisions.name: Startup Founder IP Assignment to Company
ip_provisions.description: IP assignment provisions for startup founders assigning pre-incorporation and early-stage IP to the company entity. Covers assignment of inventions conceived before incorporation, pre-existing code and designs, domain names and social media accounts, and customer relationships built during bootstrapping phase.
drafting_checklist: Define scope of pre-incorporation IP to be assigned
Specify consideration for assignment (equity, nominal payment, or both)
Address pre-existing IP developed before the business concept
Clarify assignment of domain names, social media accounts, and branding
Define timing: retroactive assignment to company formation date
Address co-founder disputes and IP allocation between founders
Specify representations that assigned IP is free of encumbrances
Define ongoing assignment obligation for founder inventions during tenure
Address vesting schedule interaction with IP assignment
Specify IP reversion rights if founder is involuntarily terminated

buyer_risks: Pre-incorporation IP may have unclear ownership (prior employer claims)
Founder may retain unassigned IP that becomes critical to the business
Vesting schedule disputes may complicate IP ownership during early stage
Co-founder departure may create IP ownership gaps
seller_risks: Broad assignment may capture personal projects unrelated to the startup
Nominal consideration may be challenged as inadequate in some jurisdictions
Involuntary termination may result in loss of personally-developed IP
Prior employer IP contamination may create personal liability

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Employee invention compensation requirements in some member states (Germany, France); founder IP assignment may require notarization; company formation varies by jurisdiction
jurisdiction_flags.US: State law governs assignment validity; California Lab. Code 2870 limits employer IP claims; Delaware preferred for IP holding; present assignment language ('hereby assigns') preferred
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Common law assignment requires consideration; company formation does not automatically vest IP; founder-director duties may affect IP assignment obligations

ip_provisions.name: Gig Worker and Platform Contractor IP Ownership
ip_provisions.description: IP ownership provisions for gig economy workers, platform-based contractors, and on-demand service providers. Addresses the classification challenge where worker status determines IP ownership defaults. Covers deliverables created through digital labor platforms, IP created using platform tools, and the intersection of worker misclassification with IP assignment validity.
drafting_checklist: Assess worker classification status before drafting IP terms
Define IP ownership independent of employment classification
Address platform terms of service interaction with direct IP agreements
Clarify ownership when multiple platforms or clients receive similar deliverables
Define IP ownership for deliverables created using platform-provided tools
Address ownership of ratings, reviews, and reputation data as IP
Specify rights to methodologies and processes developed across multiple gigs
Define scope of IP assignment relative to specific project deliverables only
Address ownership implications if worker is reclassified as employee
Specify IP ownership survival provisions independent of classification disputes

buyer_risks: Worker misclassification may invalidate work-for-hire IP ownership
Platform terms may claim rights to deliverables created through the platform
Same deliverable pattern may be provided to competing clients
IP assignment may be challenged if consideration is deemed inadequate for contractor
seller_risks: Broad IP assignment captures methodologies reusable across all clients
Multiple overlapping assignments may create conflicting IP obligations
Platform may claim ownership of work performed through its systems
Reclassification as employee may retroactively change IP ownership defaults

jurisdiction_flags.EU: Platform Work Directive creates presumption of employment; member state variation in gig worker classification; work-for-hire not recognized in most EU jurisdictions; IP ownership follows employment status
jurisdiction_flags.US: ABC test vs. common law test for classification varies by state; California AB5 and Dynamex impact gig worker IP; work-for-hire requires employee status or written agreement for enumerated categories
jurisdiction_flags.UK: Worker vs. employee vs. self-employed trichotomy; Uber BV v Aslam worker status implications; IP ownership differs between categories; IR35 off-payroll rules affect contractor IP arrangements
